key_insight: Flash austenitization in medium-Mn steel needs a brief isothermal hold to finish austenite formation, with required time dropping from about 8 s at 850°C to about 2 s at 950°C.
engineering_relevance: This gives a practical time–temperature window for designing rapid heat treatments that form stable austenite without excessive grain growth, relevant to energy infrastructure steels.
method: Dilatometry-integrated in situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction
Heated at 100°C/s to 850°C, 900°C, or 950°C, then held isothermally
evidence_signal: The abstract reports quantitative phase-fraction kinetics, showing full austenitization only when the bcc fraction falls to fα ≤ 1 wt.% after a short hold that becomes shorter at higher FA temperature.
watch_item: Results are specific to one alloy composition and the tested heating/holding schedule, so transfer to other MMn steels or larger-scale processing may require re-calibration.
paper_title: In situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction study of flash austenitization and process design insights in medium-Manganese steels for energy applications
